source: DDInter 2.0 / WHO Essential Medicines List — curated high-severity pairs

interactions.description: Increased risk of bleeding; aspirin inhibits platelet function and can displace warfarin from protein binding, potentiating anticoagulant effect.
NSAIDs inhibit platelet aggregation and may cause gastrointestinal bleeding, significantly increasing haemorrhagic risk in patients on warfarin.
NSAIDs combined with warfarin substantially increase risk of gastrointestinal and other haemorrhage.
Diclofenac inhibits platelet function; combined with warfarin increases serious bleeding risk.
Metronidazole inhibits CYP2C9, reducing warfarin metabolism and markedly elevating INR.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, substantially increasing warfarin plasma levels and INR.
Amiodarone inhibits CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, dramatically increasing warfarin effect. INR can double or triple.
Ciprofloxacin may inhibit warfarin metabolism and reduce intestinal vitamin K synthesis, elevating INR.
Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4, reducing warfarin clearance and increasing anticoagulant effect.
Clarithromycin is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor that can significantly increase warfarin levels.
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole inhibits CYP2C9 and may reduce vitamin K production, potentiating warfarin.
Initially phenytoin may increase warfarin effect; with chronic use induces CYP metabolism reducing warfarin. INR monitoring essential.
Rifampicin is a potent CYP inducer; markedly reduces warfarin plasma levels, requiring dose increases up to 5-fold.
Carbamazepine induces CYP2C9 and CYP3A4, substantially reducing warfarin levels and anticoagulant effect.
Omeprazole may modestly increase warfarin levels via CYP2C19 inhibition; INR should be monitored.
Alcohol potentiates metformin-induced lactic acidosis risk, particularly with heavy drinking.
Iodinated contrast agents can cause renal impairment, leading to metformin accumulation and lactic acidosis. Withhold metformin 48h before contrast.
Cimetidine inhibits renal tubular secretion of metformin, increasing plasma concentrations and risk of lactic acidosis.
Amiodarone inhibits CYP3A4 and CYP2C9, causing significant increases in simvastatin plasma levels and myopathy risk.
Clarithromycin markedly inhibits CYP3A4, causing very high simvastatin levels and severe risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis.
Erythromycin substantially inhibits simvastatin metabolism, significantly increasing myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk.
Fluconazole markedly increases simvastatin levels via CYP3A4 inhibition; avoid or use lowest simvastatin dose.
Itraconazole causes very large increases in simvastatin plasma levels; combination contraindicated due to rhabdomyolysis risk.
Gemfibrozil inhibits simvastatin metabolism and increases statin muscle toxicity; combination is contraindicated.
CYP3A4 inhibition by clarithromycin increases atorvastatin levels significantly, raising myopathy risk.
Itraconazole markedly increases atorvastatin exposure; should be avoided or dose reduced.
Combination increases risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis; prefer fenofibrate if fibrate required.
Amiodarone increases digoxin plasma concentration 50–100% by reducing renal and non-renal clearance. Risk of digoxin toxicity.
Spironolactone may increase digoxin levels and interfere with digoxin assays.
Clarithromycin inhibits P-glycoprotein efflux of digoxin, significantly increasing plasma digoxin levels.
Erythromycin inhibits P-glycoprotein, reducing digoxin elimination and increasing plasma concentrations.
Verapamil substantially increases digoxin plasma levels (by 40–70%) via P-glycoprotein inhibition and reduced renal clearance.
Quinidine doubles digoxin plasma levels by displacing from tissue binding sites and reducing renal clearance.
Rifampicin is a potent CYP inducer that substantially reduces phenytoin plasma levels, risking loss of seizure control.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, significantly increasing phenytoin levels and risk of toxicity.
Omeprazole can increase phenytoin levels in some patients via CYP2C19 inhibition.
Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4, increasing carbamazepine plasma levels and risk of toxicity (diplopia, ataxia, vomiting).
Clarithromycin markedly inhibits carbamazepine metabolism, causing severe carbamazepine toxicity.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP3A4, increasing carbamazepine exposure and risk of adverse effects.
Verapamil inhibits CYP3A4, substantially increasing carbamazepine levels and toxicity risk.
Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 and reduces plasma levels of oestrogen and progestogen, potentially causing contraceptive failure.
NSAIDs reduce renal lithium clearance, causing lithium toxicity. Avoid combination; monitor closely if unavoidable.
NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin-mediated renal blood flow, impairing lithium excretion and causing toxicity.
Diclofenac reduces renal lithium clearance substantially, leading to elevated lithium levels and potential toxicity.
Thiazides reduce renal lithium clearance via sodium depletion-induced sodium/lithium reabsorption; can cause lithium toxicity.
Loop diuretics can reduce lithium clearance; risk is lower than thiazides but monitoring is required.
ACE inhibitors reduce renal lithium clearance substantially, causing significant lithium toxicity.
ACE inhibitors impair renal lithium excretion; lithium levels can rise 2–3 fold.
Serotonin syndrome risk: both agents increase serotonin activity. Combination can cause agitation, hyperthermia, and autonomic instability.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6-mediated tramadol metabolism and combined serotonergic effects substantially raise serotonin syndrome risk.
Sertraline and tramadol both increase serotonin activity; serotonin syndrome is a serious risk.
Linezolid is a monoamine oxidase inhibitor; combined with fluoxetine, severe serotonin syndrome and hypertensive crisis can occur.
Combination of any SSRI with linezolid (MAO inhibitor) is contraindicated due to risk of fatal serotonin syndrome.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6-mediated pimozide metabolism and both prolong QT; combination is contraindicated.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6, significantly increasing haloperidol plasma levels and risk of extrapyramidal effects and QT prolongation.
Both are dopamine antagonists; additive risk of extrapyramidal adverse effects and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Amiodarone inhibits CYP2D6 and CYP3A4, raising metoprolol plasma levels 2–3 fold. Risk of severe bradycardia and heart block.
Amiodarone significantly inhibits simvastatin metabolism via CYP3A4 and CYP2C8; myopathy risk is substantially increased.
Amiodarone inhibits CYP3A4, markedly increasing ciclosporin plasma concentrations and nephrotoxicity risk.
NSAIDs reduce glomerular filtration rate and can precipitate acute renal failure in ciclosporin-treated patients.
Ciclosporin inhibits OATP1B1 and CYP3A4, causing very large increases in simvastatin levels and severe myopathy risk.
Fluconazole markedly inhibits ciclosporin metabolism, causing large increases in ciclosporin levels and nephrotoxicity.
Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, dramatically reducing ciclosporin levels and risking organ rejection.
Erythromycin inhibits ciclosporin metabolism; ciclosporin levels can increase 2–4 fold with nephrotoxicity risk.
Furosemide potentiates the nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity of aminoglycosides. Concurrent use should be carefully monitored.
Combination of two nephrotoxic agents significantly increases risk of acute kidney injury.
NSAIDs reduce renal methotrexate elimination, causing toxic methotrexate accumulation and bone marrow suppression.
Trimethoprim inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, enhancing methotrexate toxicity and risk of severe bone marrow suppression.
Aspirin and other NSAIDs reduce renal methotrexate clearance; serious haematological toxicity can result.
Combined hepatotoxicity risk; both agents can cause liver injury. Liver function should be monitored during TB therapy.
Both isoniazid and alcohol are hepatotoxic; combination markedly increases risk of hepatitis and liver failure.
Isoniazid inhibits CYP2C9/2C19, causing significant increases in phenytoin plasma levels and toxicity.
Rifampicin is a potent enzyme inducer that dramatically reduces plasma levels of oestrogen and progestogen; contraceptive failure is likely.
Rifampicin markedly reduces fluconazole plasma levels via CYP induction; antifungal efficacy may be lost.
Rifampicin induces CYP3A4, substantially reducing methadone levels, potentially precipitating opioid withdrawal.
Rifampicin markedly reduces verapamil plasma levels by inducing CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein; antihypertensive effect may be lost.
Rifampicin substantially reduces doxycycline plasma concentrations via CYP induction; use an alternative antibiotic.
ACE inhibitor with potassium-sparing diuretic: high risk of life-threatening hyperkalaemia, especially in renal impairment.
ACE inhibitor with spironolactone: significantly increased risk of hyperkalaemia; potassium levels must be closely monitored.
ACE inhibitors impair renal potassium excretion; concurrent potassium supplementation substantially increases hyperkalaemia risk.
NSAIDs attenuate ACE inhibitor antihypertensive effect and increase risk of acute renal failure via reduced GFR.
Combination increases risk of hypersensitivity reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome and anaphylaxis.
Amlodipine inhibits CYP3A4 and increases simvastatin exposure; limit simvastatin dose to 20 mg daily.
Combination of calcium channel blocker and beta-blocker can cause severe bradycardia, heart block, and hypotension.
Verapamil inhibits carbamazepine metabolism via CYP3A4; carbamazepine toxicity risk substantially increased.
Verapamil increases digoxin levels 40–70% via P-glycoprotein inhibition and reduced renal clearance.
Rifampicin dramatically reduces amlodipine plasma levels via CYP3A4 induction; antihypertensive effect may be abolished.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2D6, significantly increasing metoprolol plasma levels. Bradycardia and heart failure exacerbation reported.
Combination causes additive negative chronotropic and dromotropic effects; severe bradycardia and AV block risk.
Ibuprofen can competitively inhibit aspirin's irreversible platelet COX-1 inhibition, reducing the cardioprotective effect of low-dose aspirin.
Both inhibit platelet aggregation by different mechanisms; combination markedly increases bleeding risk but is sometimes intentional (dual antiplatelet therapy).
Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19-mediated activation of clopidogrel, reducing its antiplatelet effect by up to 40%.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP2C19, impairing clopidogrel bioactivation and reducing its antiplatelet effect.
Antacids reduce chloroquine absorption by up to 30%. Separate administration by at least 4 hours.
Both prolong the QT interval; combination substantially increases risk of life-threatening arrhythmias.
Both prolong QTc interval; combination increases risk of torsades de pointes and fatal arrhythmia.
Both prolong QTc interval; combination substantially increases risk of serious ventricular arrhythmia.
Quinine inhibits P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4, raising digoxin plasma levels significantly.
Both prolong QT when combined with other QT-prolonging drugs including antifungals and antipsychotics; avoid concurrent QT-prolonging agents.
Fluconazole markedly inhibits simvastatin metabolism via CYP3A4; risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis is substantially increased.
Itraconazole causes very large (up to 20-fold) increases in simvastatin exposure; risk of rhabdomyolysis is very high.
Ketoconazole markedly inhibits CYP3A4, causing major increases in simvastatin levels. Myopathy and rhabdomyolysis risk is high.
Fluconazole markedly inhibits ciclosporin metabolism, causing large increases in ciclosporin levels and nephrotoxicity risk.
Ketoconazole causes very large increases in midazolam plasma levels (15-fold); profound and prolonged sedation or respiratory depression risk.
Both prolong QT interval; erythromycin also inhibits pimozide metabolism via CYP3A4. Fatal arrhythmia risk.
Erythromycin inhibits terfenadine metabolism; both prolong QT interval. Risk of fatal torsades de pointes.
Antacids containing magnesium, aluminium or calcium substantially reduce ciprofloxacin absorption (up to 90%). Separate by at least 2 hours.
Oral iron reduces ciprofloxacin absorption by up to 90% due to chelation. Separate administration by at least 2 hours.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, increasing theophylline plasma concentrations substantially. Theophylline toxicity (nausea, arrhythmia, seizures) may result.
Ciprofloxacin may inhibit warfarin metabolism and reduce gut vitamin K synthesis, elevating INR. Monitor closely.
Divalent and trivalent cations (calcium, magnesium, aluminium, iron) chelate tetracycline, reducing absorption up to 90%.
Iron forms insoluble complexes with tetracycline, reducing absorption of both. Separate by at least 2 hours.
Antacids substantially reduce doxycycline absorption via chelation. Separate by at least 2 hours.
Iron substantially reduces doxycycline absorption. Separate by at least 2 hours.
Antacids with divalent cations chelate fluoroquinolones, dramatically reducing antibiotic absorption.
Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2, substantially increasing theophylline levels. Risk of theophylline toxicity is high.
Erythromycin inhibits CYP1A2 and 3A4, raising theophylline plasma levels with risk of cardiac arrhythmia and seizures.
Fluoxetine inhibits CYP1A2, potentially increasing theophylline plasma levels.
Both prolong QTc interval; combination substantially increases risk of torsades de pointes and sudden cardiac death.
Additive dopamine antagonism substantially increases extrapyramidal side effect risk and risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Antipsychotics and lithium can cause neurotoxicity even at normal lithium levels. Risk of encephalopathy, neuroleptic malignant syndrome.
Rare but serious neurotoxicity including irreversible brain damage has been reported, particularly at higher lithium levels.
Serotonin syndrome risk due to additive serotonergic effects. Also seizure risk with tramadol is increased by SSRIs.
Additive serotonergic effects between tramadol and sertraline. Serotonin syndrome and lowered seizure threshold risk.
Fluoxetine inhibits tramadol CYP2D6-mediated metabolism and combines serotonergic effects; serious interaction risk.
Combined central nervous system and respiratory depression substantially increases risk of respiratory arrest and death.
Concurrent opioid and benzodiazepine use substantially increases risk of respiratory depression and death.
Concurrent use increases respiratory depression, sedation, and risk of fatal respiratory arrest.
Additive CNS and respiratory depression; concurrent use substantially increases risk of death.
Metronidazole inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase; alcohol consumption causes disulfiram-like reaction with severe nausea, vomiting, flushing and tachycardia.
Tinidazole causes disulfiram-like reaction with alcohol; avoid all alcohol during treatment and for 3 days after.
Additive CNS depression substantially increases sedation and risk of respiratory failure.
Both are CNS depressants; additive sedation, respiratory depression, and risk of coma.
Fluconazole markedly inhibits CYP3A4-mediated midazolam metabolism, causing profound prolonged sedation.
Itraconazole causes very large increases in oral midazolam exposure; profound sedation and respiratory depression risk.
Loop diuretics potentiate aminoglycoside ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity via synergistic mechanisms.
Concurrent loop diuretic and aminoglycoside use substantially increases risk of irreversible hearing loss and nephrotoxicity.
Thiazide-induced sodium depletion causes compensatory lithium reabsorption, leading to lithium toxicity.
NSAIDs can attenuate thiazide diuretic efficacy and increase risk of renal impairment.
Combination carries high risk of hyperkalaemia, especially in patients with heart failure or diabetes.
Spironolactone retains potassium; adding oral potassium supplements substantially increases hyperkalaemia risk.
Oral iron chelates tetracycline, reducing antibiotic absorption by up to 90%. Separate by at least 2 hours.
Oral iron chelates ciprofloxacin; separate administration by at least 2 hours.
Iron chelates levodopa, reducing its absorption by up to 50% and affecting Parkinson disease control.
Iron chelates methyldopa, reducing its absorption and potentially impairing antihypertensive effect.
Calcium carbonate forms insoluble complexes with levothyroxine, reducing absorption by up to 40%. Separate by at least 4 hours.
Iron salts impair levothyroxine absorption substantially. Separate administration by at least 4 hours.
Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19 and reduces clopidogrel antiplatelet activation; cardiovascular risk may be increased.
Proton pump inhibitors reduce methotrexate renal tubular secretion, increasing methotrexate exposure and toxicity risk.
Combined corticosteroid and NSAID use substantially increases risk of gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding.
Corticosteroids reduce salicylate plasma levels and increase GI bleeding risk when combined.
Systemic corticosteroids may impair contraceptive efficacy by altering oestrogen metabolism.
Rifampicin substantially induces CYP3A4, reducing dexamethasone plasma levels by up to 50%. Steroid doses may need adjustment.
Additive gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding risk; consider prophylactic gastric protection.
Aspirin (even low-dose) substantially antagonises the uricosuric effect of probenecid by competing for renal tubular secretion.
Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase which metabolises azathioprine; combination causes severe azathioprine toxicity (myelosuppression). Reduce azathioprine dose by 75% if combination unavoidable.
Allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase, dramatically increasing mercaptopurine levels; severe bone marrow suppression risk.
Allopurinol inhibits azathioprine metabolism; combination contraindicated unless azathioprine dose reduced by 75%.
Zinc substantially reduces ciprofloxacin absorption via chelation. Separate by at least 2 hours.
Zinc reduces doxycycline absorption by chelation. Separate by at least 2 hours.
Zinc chelates fluoroquinolones; substantially reduces antibiotic bioavailability.
Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) accelerates peripheral decarboxylation of levodopa, markedly reducing its CNS availability when given without a decarboxylase inhibitor.
Phenobarbital induces CYP3A4 and reduces contraceptive steroid plasma levels substantially; contraceptive failure risk is high.
Phenobarbital induces CYP enzymes responsible for warfarin metabolism, substantially reducing anticoagulant effect. Higher warfarin doses required.
Combined induction of CYP enzymes; both affect multiple drug metabolisms. Monitor carefully.
Valproate inhibits lamotrigine glucuronidation, approximately doubling lamotrigine plasma levels. Lamotrigine dose must be halved.
Aspirin displaces valproate from protein binding and inhibits its beta-oxidation metabolism, increasing free valproate levels.
Valproate displaces phenytoin from protein binding and inhibits its metabolism; total phenytoin level may fall while free level rises. Risk of toxicity.
Chronic alcohol use induces CYP2E1, increasing toxic paracetamol metabolite (NAPQI) production. Risk of hepatotoxicity at therapeutic doses.
Chronic heavy alcohol consumption increases risk of paracetamol hepatotoxicity; use lowest effective dose and avoid prolonged use.
Regular paracetamol use can modestly potentiate warfarin anticoagulation; monitor INR if taking >2g/day regularly.
Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein, causing very large increases in colchicine plasma levels. Fatal colchicine toxicity has been reported.
Erythromycin inhibits P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4, substantially increasing colchicine levels and toxicity risk.
Ciclosporin inhibits P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4, substantially increasing colchicine exposure with risk of serious toxicity.
Beta blockers mask tachycardia (early warning of hypoglycaemia) and delay glucose recovery. Cardioselective beta blockers are safer but not risk-free.
Alcohol inhibits hepatic glucose production and potentiates insulin-induced hypoglycaemia; hypoglycaemia may be severe and prolonged.
Alcohol potentiates sulphonylurea-induced hypoglycaemia and may cause disulfiram-like reaction with some sulphonylureas.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9-mediated glipizide metabolism, increasing sulphonylurea levels and hypoglycaemia risk.
Fluconazole inhibits glibenclamide metabolism, substantially increasing plasma levels and hypoglycaemia risk.
Rifampicin induces CYP3A4 and substantially reduces oral contraceptive plasma levels; contraceptive failure is likely.
Carbamazepine is a strong CYP3A4 inducer; oral contraceptive efficacy is substantially reduced.
Phenytoin reduces oral contraceptive plasma levels via enzyme induction; contraceptive failure risk is high.
Phenobarbital induces contraceptive steroid metabolism; contraceptive failure risk.
Combination can cause uterine hyperstimulation and rupture, fetal distress, and placental abruption.
Phenobarbital induces metronidazole metabolism, reducing metronidazole plasma levels and potentially reducing efficacy.
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole inhibits CYP2C9 and reduces vitamin K gut production, markedly potentiating warfarin.
Both inhibit dihydrofolate reductase; additive antifolate toxicity including severe bone marrow suppression.
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole may inhibit CYP2C9, increasing phenytoin levels.
Additive myelosuppression risk; both agents suppress bone marrow function.
Sulfonamides inhibit CYP2C9-mediated warfarin metabolism and potentiate anticoagulation substantially.
Penicillins reduce renal methotrexate elimination, potentially causing haematological and gastrointestinal toxicity.
Penicillins impair methotrexate renal secretion; methotrexate levels and toxicity can be substantially increased.
Both prolong QT interval and have additive cardiac toxicity; combination increases risk of serious arrhythmias.
Clindamycin enhances neuromuscular blockade; respiratory paralysis has been reported. Caution in surgical patients.
Magnesium potentiates the negative inotropic effect of calcium channel blockers; may cause severe hypotension or respiratory arrest.
Epinephrine in patients on non-selective beta blockers can cause paradoxical hypertension via unopposed alpha-adrenergic stimulation.
Both sildenafil and nitrates cause vasodilation via NO pathway; combination can cause severe, life-threatening hypotension.
Combination of PDE5 inhibitor and nitrate causes profound hypotension; absolutely contraindicated.
Combination contraindicated; causes severe hypotension due to additive vasodilation via NO/cGMP pathway.
